data.title: Keep An Eye On Sidus Space: The Next Space Player Gaining Traction
data.description:
Thesis

When it comes to Sidus Space Inc. (SIDU), we’re currently seeing progression across LizzieSat-1, 2, and 3, and for me, it’s one of the clearest indicators that Sidus is actually moving beyond concept. What we want to see is operational capability going forward; hitting those milestones should lead to positive stock reactions. For example, LizzieSat-1 served as a very good foundational proof point and went a long way in validating their core systems like communications, payload integration, and mission control, and that was even while supporting a NASA-related mission. Essentially, this told me that they can build and operate a functioning satellite. As for LizzieSat-2, that then pushed their capability further by operating in a more challenging equatorial orbit, which basically tests their ability to manage satellites under constrained communication conditions and across different orbital environments.

But most importantly, going into 2026, LizzieSat-3 is the inflexion point here in that it has completed commissioning and is actively generating real, usable customer data, including maritime tracking and imaging. It's a massive step from testing hardware to delivering live data services and also a very critical one since it basically marks the transition from a development-stage company into an operational, revenue-capable space platform. This would be the upside buy case for Sidus and is exactly what their long-term business model depends on.

End-of-year earnings

Sidus reported FY25 financials at the start of the month, and on initial viewing, it would seem like the company is in an investment-heavy scaling phase. Revenue declined by about 28% year-over-year to $3.4 million, which would seem quite concerning; however, management clearly seems to be framing this as a deliberate transition away from lower-margin engineering services toward higher-margin satellite manufacturing. I think the more telling figure here is the cost of revenue, which jumped 48% to $9.1 million. We can put that mainly down to depreciation from the growing LizzieSat constellation and a good amount of labour needed to support on-orbit operations. So this mismatch pushed gross margins pretty negative at -168%, which is a sharp drop from the -31% margin we saw back in 2024. In practical terms, it seems the company is front-loading the costs of building a space-based data platform without actually yet realising the recurring revenue those assets are intended to generate. Now, that's rather typical of early-stage space infrastructure plays, but it does mean the current income statement is a poor indicator of long-term earnings potential.

As for operating expenses, SG&A rose to $22.3 million or about $17.8 million if we exclude a $4.5 million impairment. There was higher payroll, mission control expansion, and some business development efforts. We can combine this with increased depreciation and other non-cash charges to further drive the net loss to $29.5 million, up 68% year-over-year, and adjusted EBITDA loss to $17.3 million. However, the balance sheet tells me a different story here, and a much more supportive one.

Cash increased a lot to $43.2 million, up from $15.7 million, largely due to equity raises, and working capital improved by over 300%. It's a pretty decent liquidity position and should give Sidus a meaningful runway to continue deploying satellites like the LizzieSat-4 and -5. They also need to be in a position to pursue large contracts going forward, including defence IDIQs and lunar infrastructure deals. In that sense, the trade-off for investors is clear: Sidus has strengthened its strategic positioning/capital base, but it now has to show us that its growing satellite fleet with AI-enabled data services can actually translate into scalable, high-margin revenue before this hefty cash burn erodes that advantage.

In terms of that cash burn, even though Sidus’ balance sheet coming out of FY25 was pretty strong, we should weigh up the expected spend this year. If we take their operating expenses in SG&A and the cost of revenue as a rough proxy for cash burn here, it means the company spent about $31 to $32 million in combined operating outlays last year. Just to note, this would exclude depreciation and non‑cash charges. So it would suggest a bare‑bones runway in the 12 to 16 months at the current scale before those operating cash reserves alone are exhausted. Yes, this is a very rough calculation, and it doesn’t incorporate their working capital changes or potential revenue inflows in the near term. But it does highlight why the current cash balance is pretty important going forward, and that I don't see too much risk of dilution.

Satellite transition

As you know, Sidus is trying to set itself up not just as a satellite company, but rather as a vertically integrated space data platform. In that sense, they're aiming to design, build, launch, and then operate their satellites all within a single ecosystem. Currently, they make money in a few ways, including manufacturing satellites and components for government and commercial customers. They also host some third-party payloads on their satellites as a payload-as-a-service model. However, what they're really after is selling data and analytics generated from their own satellite constellation. This is where management sees the opportunity, and the shift we’re seeing in the financials is them moving away from the prior jobs I mentioned toward the last one in higher-margin, recurring data revenue. So this is short-term, but investment appears to be surging.

Now, at the core of that strategy is their LizzieSat constellation. This is essentially a growing network of relatively small, modular satellites in low Earth orbit. They’re actually designed to be multi-mission platforms, in that they can carry a lot of different sensors such as imaging, radio frequency, and maritime tracking. They’re also tipped to support multiple customers at once, as is usually the case with satellite data subscription services. However, there are a lot of other players in this segment with constellations far more developed. For example, Planet Labs has a diverse mix of satellites for different uses, whilst BlackSky has their Gen-3 constellation with extremely high resolution and revisit rates. So if Sidus wants to compete, having a functioning satellite constellation is the bare minimum. What they need is differentiation. That being said, their satellites are built with a software-defined, modular architecture, in the sense that they can be updated and re-tasked after launch rather than becoming obsolete; and second, they are also designed for some quite high revisit rates and continuous coverage. So the target group here seems to be more towards critical for applications like defence surveillance. In terms of defence contracts, what customers really want is speed of data transfer to insights, so revisit rates are key. Having this would allow Sidus to move more toward a constellation-as-a-service model, in which customers don’t buy a satellite, but they subscribe to capabilities or data streams to best suit their needs.

Orlaith AI

Now, as is usually the case with any small-cap defence player, they’re playing around a lot with AI integrations. This seems like a word always thrown out by management to enhance their products, but it's worth digging a little deeper into what their edge computing can offer compared to the likes of BlackSky (BKSY) with Spectre and Planet Labs (PL) with Owl. So their real differentiator here would be the heavy push into on-orbit AI. As you know, traditionally, satellites collect raw data, such as images and then send it down to Earth to be processed. Sidus wants to reduce that data interpretation timeline as much as possible. To do this, their satellites use the Orlaith AI ecosystem.

It’s powered by onboard processors such as FeatherEdge to analyse data directly in space. One big advantage here is that instead of transmitting massive raw datasets, the satellite can detect things like ships, anomalies, or targets in real time and then send only the specific relevant insights. In that sense, it does reduce latency quite a bit, but clients will be dependent on how effectively Orlaith can discriminate between which data is relevant. Bandwidth costs and response time would also be reduced. It also allows for what I call autonomous satellite behaviour, in that the satellite prioritises what to image and updates its algorithms in orbit. Again, this leads back to what I said, that when doing this, their platform will be judged on how effectively it targets specific data to send back. However, in saying that, there is some flexibility to run third-party AI models depending on mission needs.

So the transition underway is a classic but pretty ambitious one, and very similar to the likes of those other two satellite players I mentioned. They’re aiming to go from a project-based aerospace contractor to a recurring-revenue data and infrastructure company in space. So instead of getting paid once to build hardware, Sidus wants to own the satellites, continuously collect data, process it with their AI, and then sell ongoing subscriptions to intelligence and analytics. Now, this all sounds very good on paper, and it works; margins and scalability should improve dramatically, but realistically, it puts a very long road to profitability in front of them. That business model is extremely expensive. Planet Labs, for example, operates one of the largest fleets of Earth-imaging satellites with a very diverse mix of satellite types and is yet to be profitable. BlackSky, on the other hand, has had to acquire the company that builds the Gen-3 satellite and has spent years going through multiple satellite launches with Rocket Lab (RKLB) to get them up. This doesn't come cheap, and as is the case, we usually see a massive spend with a healthy growing backlog if demand is there, but revenue always seems to lag and take time to catch up. This is exactly what we’re starting to see with Sidus.

Valuation

As far as the valuation goes, Sidus would be best understood via revenue-based multiples rather than earnings. The company is still generating some hefty losses. On that basis, the stock would screen as pretty expensive, trading at roughly 22.6x trailing sales and about 19x forward sales, compared to a sector median closer to the 2x range. Elsewhere, their EV-to-sales multiple sits at about 50.6x trailing and would seem even more stretched. Again, this would be from their low current revenue base and the premium investors are assigning to its future potential. Even if we adjust for the strong net cash position, the valuation still remains elevated relative to industrial and space peers.

What I see the market pricing in here is a sharp inflexion in revenue and margin profile over the next few years. You see, investors are betting on Sidus’ transition into a high-margin, recurring revenue model with the key driver being their LizzieSat constellation. It means the implied narrative is that today’s $3 to 4 million revenue base could expand as satellite capacity comes online and contracts start converting into steady cash flow. So, currently, the set-up seems a lot like a venture-style valuation in public markets, with the stock price reflecting anticipated platform economics and long-term optionality here. It does create significant upside if execution is strong, but on the flip side, it also leaves little room for delays or under-delivery, given how much future growth is already embedded in those multiples.

Risks

I do see a few material risks here that we need to weigh up carefully. First, execution risk is the biggest thing that comes to mind. We know that the company is in an important transition phase, and one of the biggest hurdles is getting a constellation up and online. In that sense, we don't want to see delays/failures in commissioning LizzieSat-4 and -5. We also don't want to see a delay in the conversion of partnerships into signed contracts. The latter here could materially delay revenue realisation. Second, even with $43.2 million in cash, operating expenses are running quite high, and without accelerating recurring revenue at the same time associated with dilution, it may be necessary. Although I don't see this as too much of a concern with the sector tailwinds. There is also the competitive side to think about. As you know, the space surveillance industry has a lot of players. However, only a few compete at the top in that they have very advanced constellations with AI incorporations. There are numerous ways to compete and differentiate here, but some things need to be set in stone. This would include competitive revisit rates and resolution numbers. The time to deliver insights and actionable recommendations based on the data also needs to be prioritised.

Looking ahead: Thoughts on the transition

As far as the next 12 to 18 months go, they’re pretty critical. In my view, they will determine whether Sidus can convert some of the technical progress into a viable/scalable business. We have the upcoming launches of LizzieSat-4 and LizzieSat-5 as key catalysts and are particularly important to build that business model since they represent the next generation of its platform. It’s more advanced, software-defined, and has been designed from the outset specifically to compete on AI-driven, multi-mission operations. Elsewhere, we need to see that the company can take their growing list of partnerships and MOUs and convert them to signed, revenue-generating contracts. This is where real validation occurs.

What’s most important, perhaps, is the growth in recurring data revenue going forward. This makes up the long-term thesis, in that moving from one-off hardware sales to continuous, high-margin data and analytics streams can actually pay off. We can put on top of this the opportunity in defence, which is seeing good tailwinds in early 2026, and in particular, big wins under large frameworks like SHIELD, could possibly give us some scale and stability. That being said, this current fiscal year is less about proving the technology and more about proving the whole business model. So execution, customer adoption, and revenue conversion will ultimately tell us if Sidus can justify its ambitious positioning.

In my view, Sidus has entered that critical inflexion point now. So upside is on execution in transitioning from a development-stage company into an operational, revenue-capable space platform. This is the correct path to take and clearly where the tailwinds are pointing. We have the LizzieSat-3 generating live customer data and decent progression across its constellation. Yes, revenue is still early-stage, and margins remain negative, but keep in mind that the company is moving from proving that technology to executing scalable, multi-mission operations, which isn't easy. This is essentially the foundation for the recurring revenue model that backs my long-term thesis here. Execution over the next 12 to 18 months should show us whether this inflexion can actually translate into tangible financial results.

data.title: Li Auto: Should We Be Skeptical Amid Broader Sector Recovery?
data.description:
Update since 2Q25

Li Auto (LI) has performed poorly since my last thesis in mid-2025. At the time, auto sales in China have been facing headwinds amidst a broader curb of price competition. There were firms that suffered from this, most notably BYD, given its leadership on both the cost and tech fronts. CN Auto stocks have broadly been sluggish since 2H25.

Peers

At the time of my writing, Li Auto was already faced with some slowdown (see 2Q25 transcript). But I've been more optimistic than the market has on the basis of its niche and higher-end marketing strategy, providing decent protections against sales headwinds as threats were made to lower subsidies in order to disincentivize further "rat race" among peers.

That has been proven wrong, namely that competition between peers is much more fierce and dynamic. The logic of higher-end SUV segmentation is also more nuanced, with most of its revenues actually generated from the mid-tiered L6 models. Focusing on this segment was a great idea. SUVs are naturally great sellers, targeted at larger families with greater willingness to pay. In light of that, many manufacturers have been playing catch-up—players like BYD (BYDDF), Geely (GELYF), XPeng (XPEV), NIO (NIO), etc. Battery techs have also been improving, with BEVs imposing more pressure on EREVs.

FY25 results

Unit sales had dropped by 19% to 400k units for FY25, while revenue had dropped to CNY110 million, down by 23%. Margins were squeezed significantly, with the company operating at a loss in 2H25 amid some phase-outs of subsidies and elevated competition by peers. L6 models were gradually losing their sales momentum. Higher-end models, including L8 and L9, have been experiencing more volatile declines. During the same year, Li Auto's close competitor Aito has been much more resilient, with consumers buying into the AI ecosystem offered by Huawei, ranging from its level of intelligent driving to connectivity with mobile devices.

Impact on margins

Overall, key events through the year, especially in the second half, include its deliveries of the i6 and i8 and the marginal improvements made towards its VLA model. Sales for the i-series are challenging overall, marked by product line reconfigurations and financial incentives. For automakers in China, consistent and substantial improvements in intelligent driving are table stakes. Li Auto's current achievements in autonomous driving are mostly average.

2026 outlook

In 1Q26, Li Auto had delivered 95k units, against the 93k in the same quarter last year. Moving forward, the core drivers of growth remain to be the i-series, even with the mediocre response from last year. In addition to that, the company is also betting on the success of its L9 series, competing in the premium SUV segment. Overall, management is targeting 480k-500k units for 2026. So far, they are falling short of this expectation.

Quarterly figures (2024-2025)

For Li Auto, stabilization is really at the top of priority. I don't think that one should assume that the company is in a good state of product market fit for the reasons mentioned at the beginning. As we move forward, we should be continuously monitoring its i-series, reiterated luxury vehicles like the L9, and the actual extent of price discounts. Historically, highly luxurious cars haven't done so well, as testified by the MEGA series. This is quite a challenging market to operate in. Even unit sales have been quite stable so far since the beginning of 2026; we have yet to ascertain the extent of price discounts that went into this.

Valuation

Valuations have gone more expensive than they were in the previous year due to declining sales, and even more so for earnings due to operating leverage. For my initial thesis 10 months ago, I thought its valuation was fine under the assumptions of product moat in the mid-to-high-tier family-oriented SUVs. ASPs from its higher-end EVs would make it one of the most profitable automakers.

EBITDA/EV

That said, unlevered multiples for the company are still quite low (on a forward basis), which offers some bargain as the stock price appears to have stabilized for the past quarter, right after the prolonged decline in 2H25 that had cut its capitalization by almost half, reaching $18 billion. There are several justifications for the stock stabilizing: policy uncertainties have declined significantly, Li Auto has made significant progress in BEVs and other key product updates and is in better shape than it was in mid-2025, and the fact that the company has over $14 billion in cash vs. its $18 billion market capitalization. That gives considerable optionality in improving sales, product, and potentially buybacks as the market stabilizes. Right now, $1 billion in buybacks has already been approved in 2026.

Final take

There are two realities of the company to consider. First, the majority of its products have been faced with considerable headwinds. Many of the competitors (from low- to high-end) have cracked the domestic EV SUV market. A few are having pretty good times, leaving Li Auto in the wind. The company is on a deadline to reverse sales; at the same time, it does have a strong balance sheet and cash position bolstered by the successes it has enjoyed since 2022. This gives considerable optionality.

As it had initially become a leader in the market early on, I believe that it had grown a bit complacent, whether by being over-reliant on EREVs, less diversified in products, or generally slower than peers in initiatives like speeding up intelligence development or pursuing international expansion. 2025 was the year that the intense competition had hurt results and made them realize the necessity to perform minor pivots.

2025 has also changed the premium EV SUV market landscape, with many new entrants. This elevates the competitiveness moving forward, while subsidies have toned down in 2026. Right now, management has expressed a good deal of confidence to revive earnings with plans to alter both cost and sales. The confidence was backed by the recent dividend commitment.

On the flip side, markets did, in fact, become more competitive due to new rival products and less demand by consumers. Furthermore, EREVs are faced with even more pressure amid substantial developments of LFP and solid-state batteries. In addition, 1Q26 sales performance does not seem to echo the same level of excitement by management. As a result, I am rating LI "hold" while waiting for 1Q26 results for more clarity (i.e., how much of the unit sales are driven by discounts?). What will be the actual impact on costs amid the shift in store strategy? Overall, I do think the sector should do well in 2026, as price competitions were curbed and there is more clarity on policies.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bain Capital Specialty Finance: Deep Discount, Strong Returns
data.description:
Bain Capital Specialty Finance, Inc. (BCSF), is a BDC managed by an affiliate of Bain Capital, one of the world's leading private invеstment firms. With а diversified pоrtfolio of ovеr 200 holdings across multiple industriеs and a consistеnt histоry of outperfоrming its peеr grоup in average total returns, BCSF has estаblished itself аs one of the mоre resiliеnt playеrs in the BDC spаce. Dеspite this strоng opеrating profilе, the company's common stock is currеntly trаding at onе of the deеpest historicаl discоunts to NAV, a situation that creates an attrаctive oppоrtunity for invеstors. If you want to see our last article for the company (at its 3rd quarter), you can do so here.

Assets

At the end of 2025, Bain Capital Specialty Finance, Inc. had close to $2.66 billion in total assets and $1.47 billion in total debt. The investments in fair value represent close to 94% of the total assets, and the non-accrual loans represent 1.5% and 0.8% of the total investment portfolio at amortized cost and fair value. The current market capitalization is close to $816 million. BCSF has close to 203 holdings, and the biggest part of its portfolio is in the "Investment Vehicles" industry (16%), followed by "High Tech Industries" (11%), and "Services: Business" (10%).

  • Reddit Rating

The company has a "BBB-" credit rating by Fitch, as you can see in the next screenshot.

Debt

The largest part of the company's portfolio is in "First lien senior secured" - close to 64%, followed by 16% in "Investment Vehicles", 15% in "Equity and Other", 4% in "Subordinated Debt", and 1% in "Second Lien".

The largest part of the company's debt is in Unsecured debt - close to 64%, followed by 19% in the Middle Market CLO, and 17% in the Secured Revolving Credit Facility.

And below you can see the debt distributions of BCSF for the last quarter:

Historical Performance

The five-year average annual total return on NAV is close to 10.41%, which is higher compared to the peer group's 7.18%. And the 3-year average annual total return on NAV is around 10.35%, 3.4% higher than the peer group.

Historical Premium/Discount

If we look at the historical/premium discount chart, we see that for the last 10 years, BCSF has had a few of its lowest points - currently it is at one of them. At the time of writing, the common stock is traded at a close to 27% discount, which is far away from its average discount for the last 5 years, close to 13.6%:

Expenses

The operating expenses of the company are close to 25% of the total investment income, which is close to $273.2 million at the end of 2025. With assets of around $2.66 billion, the gross assets yield is close to 10.26%. The gross-asset non-leverage expense ratio is close to 2.85%.

Comparisons

Below is shown a whole BDC universe of over-the-counter bonds:

The highest yield to maturity is close to 7.54%, but the average yield to maturity of the group is around 6%.

As we said above, the average asset yield of BCSF is around 10.26%. The average loss of yield is close to 0.73%, and for the last 3 years, the average loss/total income is around 7%. When we include all of these, the expected ROE for the common stock is around 9.28%, but because the common stock is at a nearly 27% discount, the discount-adjusted ROE increases to around 12.71%. These values ​​are derived from historical data over the past 3 years. To make the proper comparison with the bond, we add leverage to the average OTC bond and consider a hypothetical capital structure with 200 total assets and 100 net assets. Under these conditions, the expected leveraged-bond ROE is close to 8.50%. You can see the comparison below:

When comparing the common stock to a hypothetical leveraged average OTC bond, the stock emerges as the more attractive alternative. Although thе average yield to maturity of OTC bonds is around 6% (with a mаximum of 7.54%), the leverаgеd bоnd ROE of 8.50% remаins belоw the expеcted BCSF's ROE of 9.28%. The stоck's discоunt-adjustеd ROE jumps to 12.71% - significаntly highеr thаn the bоnd alternative. Although cоmmon stocks аre riskier than bоnds, the highеr expected rеturn makes this аdditional risk fully justifiеd.

Risks

Despite BCSF's strеngths, investors shоuld considеr the follоwing risks:

- Interest Rate Sensitivity: As a BDC, BCSF's revеnues are tiеd to floating intеrest rates. A rаpid dеcline in primе interеst rаtes could squeеze profit margins before debt costs are fully adjusted.

- Middle Markеt Credit Risk: BCSF invests primаrily in middlе mаrket companies. Thеse companies may be morе vulnеrable in an еconomic dоwnturn, which would incrеase the rate of nоn-performing loans.

- Markеt Volаtility and Liquidity: The currеnt discоunt of 27% reflеcts markеt sentimеnt. If macroecоnomic concеrns persist, this discоunt cоuld rеmain elevatеd for an еxtended pеriod or evеn incrеase.

Conclusion

BCSF is a finаncially sоund compаny with a proven profitability, that is currently trading at an unusually high discount. This makes the stоck mоre attrаctive thаn over-the-counter bonds of compаrable compаnies - еven aftеr accоunting fоr аll cоsts and lоsses. An investor buying today receives more rеturn (but also a higher risk exposure), while аlso bеtting on pоtential appreciation when the discоunt rеturns to histоrical lеvels.
